title: Dermoscopy Skin Lesion Malignancy Classification (PWDR)
sub_domain: Cross-polarized cutaneous reflectance imaging with melanoma / nevus categorical readout
source_data_quality_note: v2 physical_with_discrete_readout (PWDR) genesis Principle. Wraps L1-049 fundus (sibling RGB reflectance imaging analytical core, with note that dermoscopy and fundus share the surface-reflectance physics although clinical applications differ) with the established ABCDE / 7-point checklist / Menzies / CASH dermoscopy clinical-grading rules to assign categorical labels {benign nevus, suspected melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis}. Universal in dermatology and primary care; FDA-cleared autonomous AI exists (DermaSensor, MoleScope variants). UTSW Department of Dermatology runs dermoscopy clinics.

gate_substitutions.conservation_substitute: softmax simplex closure on cutaneous-lesion categorical readout

discrete_readout.threshold_function: ABCDE rule (Stolz 1994): TDS = 1.3*A + 0.1*B + 0.5*C + 0.5*D where A=asymmetry score (0-2), B=border score (0-8), C=color score (1-6), D=differential structure score (1-5); TDS < 4.75 = benign, 4.75-5.45 = suspicious, > 5.45 = melanoma. Alternative: 7-point checklist (Argenziano 1998) — 3+ points = suspect melanoma. Alternative: Menzies method, CASH algorithm, BCC blue-white veil, SCC characteristic features.
discrete_readout.underlying_physical_state: skin lesion morphological-feature recovery: asymmetry score, border irregularity, color heterogeneity (number of distinct colors), differential structures (pigment network, dots/globules, streaks, regression structures, blue-white veil) recovered from cross-polarized dermoscopy image
discrete_readout.threshold_continuity_proof: ABCDE Total Dermoscopy Score (TDS) is a Lipschitz-continuous linear combination of feature scores; per-class boundaries at TDS thresholds (4.75, 5.45) are measure-zero hyperplanes. 7-point checklist sums ordinal feature presences (Lipschitz with constant ~0.5 per feature). Smoothed-grading globally Lipschitz with constant ~0.10 per feature-score unit.

related_principles: L1-049 (fundus — sibling RGB reflectance imaging core, physics_core_principle; note: fundus is retinal, dermoscopy is cutaneous — different anatomy but shared cross-polarized-reflectance physics)
L1-064 (CLE — confocal laser endomicroscopy sibling, informational)

E.description: Dermoscopy Skin Lesion Malignancy Classification (PWDR): wraps L1-049 fundus analytical core (RGB cross-polarized reflectance imaging) — note: fundus L1-049 is geometrically retinal, but the physics core of cross-polarized RGB reflectance imaging applies to skin dermoscopy with appropriate scale and illumination adjustments — with the established ABCDE / 7-point checklist / Menzies dermoscopy clinical-grading rules. Stage 1 (analytical, from L1-049 sibling-physics): cross-polarized white-light dermoscopy recovers cutaneous lesion morphological features — asymmetry, border, color heterogeneity, differential structures (pigment network, dots, streaks, regression). Stage 2 (deterministic threshold): apply ABCDE TDS or 7-point checklist or Menzies criteria to assign categorical labels. Difficulty tier delta = 3. Mismatch parameters: dermoscope_polarization_efficiency, gel_immersion_uniformity, lesion_contrast_against_skin_background, hair_artifact, photographic_color_calibration, manual_feature_scoring_disagreement.
E.forward_model: Stage 1 (sibling to L1-049 reflectance-imaging core): cross-polarized RGB dermoscopy -> {asymmetry, border, color, structure} morphological features. Stage 2: TDS = 1.3*A + 0.1*B + 0.5*C + 0.5*D; class = step(TDS at [4.75, 5.45]); alternative 7-point sum; alternative Menzies positive/negative.
E.world_state_x: Per-lesion categorical label in {benign_nevus, atypical_nevus, suspected_melanoma, basal_cell_carcinoma, squamous_cell_carcinoma, seborrheic_keratosis, actinic_keratosis}.
E.observation_y: Cross-polarized RGB dermoscopy image (typically 3000x4000 pixel, 10-30x magnification, 50 micrometer pixel pitch).

G.dag: L.cross_polarized_illumination -> L.diffuse_reflectance -> L.detector_response -> L.morphological_feature_extraction -> L.abcde_threshold_classifier -> int.spatial
coupling_constraints: polarization_to_subsurface: cross-polarized illumination suppresses surface specular reflection and enables visualization of subsurface pigment + vascular structures; coupling between polarization state and subsurface chromophore visibility

W.regime: Existence and uniqueness conditional on adequate cross-polarization (suppresses skin glare) and sufficient lesion contrast against background skin. Stability inherits sibling-core kappa_eff plus additive contribution from manual_feature_scoring_disagreement (the dominant inter-rater variability source in clinical practice). Joint Hadamard well-posedness for the coupled dermoscopy + ABCDE/7-point/Menzies threshold forward established by Stolz 1994 (foundational ABCDE), Argenziano 1998 (7-point checklist), Menzies 1996 (Menzies method), Henning 2007 (CASH algorithm), Esteva 2017 (deep-learning benchmark).

C.solver_class: linear-operator + convex optimisation [feature-segmentation + threshold] | end-to-end deep neural [DeepDerm, DermaSensor] with explicit physics-informed feature regularization
C.error_bound: Inherits sibling-core feature-recovery bound. Misclassification probability bound: Pr(class_hat != class_true) <= C_thresh * |feature_score_error| / d(features, tau_boundary).
C.complexity: O(H * W * N_features) per stage; total stage-1-dominated

recast_attempted.recast_path: Original framing: 'Classify dermoscopy images into melanoma/benign'. Option-3 recast: 'Recover lesion morphological features (asymmetry, border, color, differential structures) via cross-polarized reflectance imaging; apply ABCDE / 7-point checklist / Menzies thresholds'. Feasible because dermoscopy features have a well-defined photometric forward model and clinical-grading rules are deterministic functions of recovered features.

joint_well_posedness_references: Stolz W et al. (1994) ABCD rule of dermoscopy: A new practical method for early recognition of malignant melanoma. Eur J Dermatol 4(7):521-527.

Argenziano G et al. (1998) Epiluminescence microscopy for the diagnosis of doubtful melanocytic skin lesions. Comparison of the ABCD rule of dermatoscopy and a new 7-point checklist. Arch Dermatol 134(12):1563-1570.
Menzies SW, Ingvar C, Crotty KA, McCarthy WH (1996) Frequency and morphologic characteristics of invasive melanomas lacking specific surface microscopic features. Arch Dermatol 132(10):1178-1182.
Henning JS et al. (2007) The CASH (color, architecture, symmetry, and homogeneity) algorithm for dermoscopy. J Am Acad Dermatol 56(1):45-52.
Esteva A et al. (2017) Dermatologist-level classification of skin cancer with deep neural networks. Nature 542:115-118.
Tschandl P, Rosendahl C, Kittler H (2018) The HAM10000 dataset, a large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. Sci Data 5:180161.

distinctness_audit.rationale: L1-049 fundus is RGB color reflectance imaging of retina; L1-517 dermoscopy is cross-polarized RGB reflectance imaging of skin. Sibling physics (both are tissue-surface RGB reflectance imaging) but different anatomy, different polarization state, different magnification regime. d_principle ~ 0.45 reflects shared core physics with anatomy / illumination / scale differences. Note: a future dedicated dermoscopy analytical core could be authored separately; for now this PWDR uses L1-049 as the closest existing reflectance-imaging Principle.

v3_metadata.clinical_context: Dermoscopy is the standard-of-care examination tool for pigmented skin lesion evaluation in dermatology and increasingly in primary care. ABCDE rule (Stolz 1994), 7-point checklist (Argenziano 1998), Menzies method (1996), and CASH algorithm (Henning 2007) are the canonical clinical-grading rules. Deep-learning systems (Esteva 2017) achieve dermatologist-level classification on benchmark datasets (HAM10000, ISIC). FDA-cleared autonomous AI: DermaSensor (2024), MelaFind (2011-2017, withdrawn). UTSW Department of Dermatology runs dermoscopy training and screening clinics. PWDR formulation gives physics-grounded explainability: classification is derived from explicit morphological feature scores tied to clinical guidelines, not black-box CNN.
